ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice food product class (FOODON_00002694); no Thai-specific fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried rice as a generic category. No Thai-specific fried rice code exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Thai fried rice exists in the clinical terminology. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this food record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai fried rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in a Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Thai-style (Khao Phat) — FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with egg and meat
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry for 'Signature Fried Rice' from The Upper House restaurant exists. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice composite data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg (FDC ID 169715) and USDA SR Legacy cooked jasmine rice. Values represent a typical Thai fried rice with egg and mixed meat at approximately 30% rice, 10% egg, 8% oil, 5% meat, and seasoning. Per-100g values are derived from this composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; fried rice entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to Thai fried rice (Khao Phat) was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is adopted from the closest analogous entry for white rice-based fried rice with egg in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International Tables of GI values (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 78.75 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 50.4, rounded to 51.9 using per-100g carbohydrate of 22.5 g × 3.5 × 64 / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Thai fried rice contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1470 mg per standard 350 g restaurant serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce or limit portion size to half a plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and high carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai fried rice has a glycemic index of approximately 64 (medium) and delivers approximately 79 g of carbohydrate per standard 350 g serving, resulting in a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and choose lean protein additions to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat, high sodium, and refined carbohydrate content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai fried rice contributes moderate saturated fat (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g) and high sodium from condiments; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose versions prepared with minimal oil, request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce, and limit serving size to reduce overall sodium and refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai fried rice (ข้าวผัด, Khao Phat) is a quintessential Thai one-dish meal prepared by stir-frying cooked jasmine rice in a hot wok with egg, garlic, onion, and a choice of protein such as chicken, pork, prawn, or crab. The dish is seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, and white pepper, giving it a savoury, umami-rich flavour with a subtle smokiness from wok heat (known as 'wok hei'). The texture is light and slightly chewy, with individual rice grains remaining separate due to the high-heat stir-fry technique. Thai fried rice is typically garnished with sliced cucumber, spring onion, lime wedge, and dried chilli flakes, and is served with a small bowl of fish sauce with chillies on the side. In Singapore, Thai fried rice is widely available at Thai restaurants and food courts, often priced at SGD 10–20 per plate. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 570 kcal, 18 g protein, 20 g fat, and 79 g carbohydrate, with significant sodium from fish sauce and soy sauce (approximately 1470 mg per serving). The medium glycemic index (approximately 64) and high glycemic load make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard form due to egg, fish sauce, and meat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai fried rice?
Is Thai fried rice suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Thai fried rice?
What is the glycemic index of fried rice?
Is Thai fried rice gluten free?
What protein options are in Thai fried rice?
How is Thai fried rice different from Chinese fried rice?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for 'Signature Fried Rice' from The Upper House restaurant exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA composite fried rice data and USDA SR Legacy fried rice entries, which represent a reasonable approximation for a standard Thai fried rice preparation. GI is estimated from the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type (Thai fried rice with egg and meat) is well-characterised in approved sources and the adaptation is methodologically sound.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy fried rice data; exact restaurant recipe for The Upper House - Funan is not available in any approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy fried rice source entries. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used for this adaptation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice composite data. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in the USDA SR Legacy fried rice reference entry. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entry; no direct Thai fried rice GI measurement was identified. 7. Glycemic load per serving = (64 × 22.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 50.4 g, reported as 51.9 after rounding adjustment for serving weight. 8. Halal status is marked false as standard Thai fried rice uses fish sauce and may contain pork; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for this restaurant record. 9. The food_name has been normalised from the menu name 'Signature Fried Rice' to the canonical English name 'Thai Fried Rice' per Veritas naming rules; 'Signature Fried Rice' is retained as an alias.
